content.full_text:
Re:
Fern Polrrrcnl PRACTTcEs CouMrssroN
428 J Street r Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA 9SBl4-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
February 2,2010
Theodore R. Meriam
Clayton Planning Commissioner
990 Oak Street
Clayton, CA94517
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our file No.I-10-203
Dear Mr. Meriam:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' We base our advice on the facts presented;
the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when
it renders advice. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice
solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-
of-interest laws. Because your questions are general in nature and do not involve specific
governmental decisions, we can provide you only with informal assistance.2
QUESTTON
Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest that would prohibit you from making or
participating in decisions as a planning commissioner based on the proximity of your primaiy
residence to a potential development project in downtown Clayton?
CONCLUSION
Your facts suggest that your property is within 500 feet of some govemmental decisions
and more than 500 feet from others. Based on the impact of the decisions, it is likely that the
decisions are reasonably foreseeable to have a material financial effect on your economic
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the piir potiticat practices
Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinign or tbrmal
written advice. (Section 831 l4; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


File No. I-10-203
Page No. 2
interest. We note however, that you must make this determination on a decision-by-decision
basis.
FACTS
You are a planning commissioner for the City of Clayton. Over the coming months, the
planning commission will be making recommendations to the Clayton City Counci regarding a
project in the downtown area of Clayton. You live in a house that is held in a non-revocable
living trust. You are the trustor and beneficiary of the trust as well as being responsible for all
payments and repairs on the house. You pay the mortgages on the property Air."tty from your
personal funds.
One of the issues before the planning commission will be whether the city should waive
downtown parking requirements that would therefore allow for additional parking within 500
feet of your property. Other issues involve the construction of a church within downtown
Clayton, but beyond 500 feet of your property. You have provided information regarding the
potential uses of the church that range from activities invoiving a few people present on a
consistent basis to services where up to 1,000 people attend. th" do"um"ntutlon regarding the
project states that the church will be at least 15,000 square feet, and the project williequeJt use
of public lots throughout the downtown area.
You have not offered any information regarding any other project or economic interest.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section g7100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 1870b@).) The geniral rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official mak"r u gou"*mental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one o, *o." of his or her
financial interests.
srBrs 1 & 2: ARr vou A punr,rc orrrcrlr, M^rr<rxc, paRucp,rrrNc rN MlrrNc, oR
INrTurxcING A GovERN}IENTAL DncTsToN?
As a member of the planning commission, you are a public official under the Act. (Section
82048.) Consequently, you may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use your official
position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on any of your economic interests. Because you will be called Llpon to consider proposed projects


File No. I-10-203
Page No. 3
and make recommendations to the city council, you will be making, participating in making, or
otherwise using your official position to influence a governmental decision.l
Srnp 3: Do You Havn A PoTENTIALLY DTsquu,mYING ECoNoIvIIC INrBnrsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material finaniial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests.
You have an interest in the real property located in downtown Clayton. Section g2033 of
the Act definite an interest in real property as:
"Interest in real property" includes any leasehold, beneficial or ownership
interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in
the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficialiy uy the public
official, or other filer, or his or her immediate family if thofair market
value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. lnterests in
real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or
immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or benefici ally, a l0-percent
interest or greater."
An official has an economic interest in the pro rata share of the interests in real property
of a trust in which the official has a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest of 10 percent or greater.
(Regulation L8%a@).) An official has a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest in a trust if the
official is:
"(1) A trustor and:
"(A) Can revoke or terminate the trust;
"(B) Has retained or reserved any rights to the income or principal of the trust,
or retained any reversionary or remainder interest; or
"(C) Has retained or reserved any power of appointment, including but not
limited to the power to change the trustee, orit " 
power to amend, alter or
designate, either alone or in conjunction with anyone else, the person or
persons who shall possess or enjoy the trust property or income.
"(2) A beneficiary and:
"(A) Presently receives income; or
I If a public otficial's office is listed in section 87200 (*87200 filers" include planning commission
members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then'he or she must: ( I )
immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic Interest involved in the
decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulition 18702.5(b)( I )(B), on the record of rhe
meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the
item' For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal
interests, special rules tbund in regulation |STO2.5,subdivisions (ciand (d) apply. (Section g7105.)


File No. I-lO-203
Page No.4
"(B) Has an irrevocable ftrture right to receive income or principal. For
purposes of this subsection, an individual has an irrevocabl" futur" right to
receive income or principal if the trust is irrevocable, unless one of the
following applies:
"(i) Powers exist to consume, invade, or appoint the principal for the
benefit of beneficiaries other than the official andsuch po*"., are not limited
by an ascertainable standard relating to the health, education, support, or
maintenance of the beneficiaries; or
"(ii) Under the terms of the trust, someone other than the official can
designate the persons who shall possess or enjoy the trust property or
income."
(Regulation I 823a@)-(c) (copy enclosed).)
You have stated that you are trustor and beneficiary of the living trust. As explained in
the regulation above, you have a beneficial interest in the irust. You have a 100 percent interest
in the trust until the time of your death and we assume that the property is worth more than
$2,000. For purposes of the Act, you have an economic interesi in this property.
You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests.
Srnp 4: Is Tsn EcoNovttc INtBRrsr Drnrcrr,y oR INDTRECTLy INvor,vpo rN THE
GovBnNmENTAL DBcrsroN?a
"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's
economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the governmental decision.,,
(Regulation 18704(a)') For govemmental decisions that affect reaiproperty interests, the
standards set forth in Regulation rg7o4.2 apply. (Regulation lg7oiiu,):e).;
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly involved in
the governmental decision if it is located within 500 feet of the property that is the subject of the
govemmental decision. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(1).) While theie is anlxception ro rh; general
rule if the decision solely concems the amendment of an existing zoning ord'iror"", as you
pointed out, the decision to come before the planning commissiJn does not seem to involve a
zoning ordinance. (Regulation 18704.2(b)(1).) The decisions you described involve.,waiving
parking restrictions" rather than re-zoning. Consequently, youi p.op"rty will be directly
involved in the decisions to waive parking restrictions that invotve iarking within 300 feet of
your property.
You have stated that the site where the church will be built is more than 500 f-eet from
your property. As stated above, property that is greater than 500 feet from the subject of a
govemmental decision is not directly involved. Your property is therefore not directly involved
in decisions regarding the church. (Regulation 19704:2d).)
t If a public otlicial's economic interest in not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considered
"indirectly involved." (Regulation I 8704(a).)


File No. l-10-203
Page No. 5
Srrp 5: MersnrAlrry SrlNnam
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)
The effect of a govemmental decision on real property that is directly involved in the
govemmental decision is presumed to be material. (Regulation 18705.2(aXi;.; yorr property is
directly involved in the decisions regarding waiving parking restrictions *iiti" 500 of your
property. The effect of these decisions is therefore presumed to be material.
For real property that is not directly involved in a governmental decision, Regulation
18705'2(b)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on real property is
presumed not to be material. That presumption may be rebutted with proof of speclfic
circumstances regarding the govemmental decision and its effect. Examples of such
circumstances include the character of the neighborhood, including effects on traffic, view,
privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions or similar traiti. (Regulation 1g705.2(bxl).)
Given the size and scope of this project, the potential impact from an increase in
automobiles and people, and the potential change of the character of the surrounding
neighborhoods, it seemsthe presumption of immateriality is rebutted regarding deciiions
involving the building of the church structure, even though it will be moie th; 500 feet from
your property.
You have not given any facts indicating that either the 'public generally" exception or
that the "legally required participation,'exception would apply.
Srnp 6: RsasoNABLy f,'onssrB.anlr
Whether the financial consequences of a govemmental decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time the decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable,-but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops. l9g.)
Ultimately, it is up to the public official to make the determination through a good faith
effort. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-l 13; MoockAdvice Letter, No. A-01-l40; O'Harra
Advice Letter, No. 4-00-174.) Given the facts as you have described them, you will need to
determine whether the decisions before the planning commission will have the effects described
above.
srrps T lNn 8: Pusrrc GrNrnu,r,y AND Lpc.lr,r,y Rrqurnro plnrrcrp.ruoN


File No. I-10-203
Page No. 6
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
//,tu*
By:
Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
HMRjgl
Enclosure

basis.
